analogy: Think of "Why Do We Fall Ill" through the chapter's own opening images: Earth as a System: Energy, Matter, and Life Think It Over y How does the warming of Arabian Sea water affect the southwest monsoon in India? y If a large forest is cleared, how can that affect the flow of a river in that area?... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: 253 Earth as a S ystem: Energy, Matter, and Life, 13.1 Uneven Heating of the Earth, 255 Earth as a System: Energy, Matter, and L ife, 13.1.1 Interaction of solar radiation on. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Why Do We Fall Ill is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that earth as a System: Energy, Matter, and Life Think It Over y How does the warming of Arabian Sea water affect the southwest monsoon in India? y If a large forest is cleared, how can that affect the flow of a river in that area? y What might happen to coastal cities in India if glaciers and polar ice keep melting faster? y How would increasing carbon dioxide levels in the atmosphere affect the ocean plankton?

The text explains that chapter 13 Life on Earth is powered by a constant flow of energy and matter.

The text explains that in addition, the Earth’s hot interior and chemical reactions in the air, water, and rocks also drive the flow of energy and matter.

The text explains that during the middle stage in Curiosity (Grades 6 – 8) and in earlier chapters of this textbook, you explored these ideas as separate content pieces.

The text explains that for example, you learnt how sunlight drives winds and the water cycle; how plants and microbes cycle nutrients; how the Earth’s tilt causes seasons; and how human activities are modifying air, water, soil and climate.

The text explains that in this chapter, we will consider these processes together as belonging to one Earth system made up of interacting ‘spheres’ — y Geosphere: Solid rocks, soil, landforms (like the Deccan plateau and the Thar desert), and the Earth’s interior. y Hydrosphere: Liquid water in the form of surface water, such as oceans, rivers (like the Ganga – Brahmaputra river system), lakes and groundwater.

The text explains that 253 Earth as a S ystem: Energy, Matter, and Life y Cryosphere: Solid form of water, such as ice and snow (like the Himalayan glaciers, snow in Ladakh and polar ice caps). y Atmosphere: The air surrounding the Earth that we breathe (cleaner air in the mountains and forests). y Biosphere: All living organisms and their habitats (including mangroves, forests, farms, ocean plankton and coral reefs).

The text explains that we will explore how energy and matter move, interact across the Earth’s spheres, and how a change in one affects others.

The text explains that understanding these processes has been an important part of the continuing journey of the exploration of science.

The text explains that natural processes, such as heating by solar radiation, movement of air and water, and nutrient cycling connect these spheres in a delicate balance.

The text explains that let us understand this by conducting Activity 13.1 and explore how different spheres on the Earth continuously interact with each other.

The text explains that observe the features of the Earth as shown in Fig.

The text explains that identify and circle one example representing each of the geosphere, hydrosphere, cryosphere, atmosphere, and biosphere.

The text explains that how does snow (cryosphere) eventually become part of the lake (hydrosphere)?

The text explains that if there is less snowfall during winters for a few years, how would this affect the lake’s level and the grass available for the sheep?

The text explains that discuss with your classmates and write down how all the spheres are interconnected, and how a disturbance in one can lead to changes in others.

The text explains that at the same time, the rise in atmospheric temperature could eventually accelerate the melting of glaciers and polar ice in the cryosphere, which may lead to the flooding of low-lying regions, and in the long run, it can raise sea levels that may threaten coastal cities.

The text explains that this could disturb the ecosystems within the biosphere by causing a habitat loss.

The text explains that does the solar radiation heat the Earth’s surface evenly?

The text explains that let us explore how solar radiation varies from the equator to the poles, the oceans to the mountains, and drives many of Earth’s natural processes, such as wind, ocean current, water cycle, etc.

The text explains that 13.1 Uneven Heating of the Earth Solar radiation is the main source of energy on the Earth.

The text explains that it reaches the Earth as electromagnetic (EM) waves that travel through a vacuum at Fig.

The text explains that exploration|Grade 9 254 the speed of light (unlike EM waves, sound waves, which you studied in Chapter 10, Sound Waves: Characteristics and Applications, are mechanical waves and require a medium to travel).

The text explains that eM waves cover a wide range of frequencies, from high frequency or short wavelength radiation (gamma rays and X-rays) to low frequency or long wavelength radiation (infrared and radio waves).

The text explains that the high frequency EM waves, such as gamma rays and X-rays have very high energy and can be harmful for life on Earth.

The text explains that however, the solar radiation reaching Earth is concentrated mainly in the ultraviolet (UV), visible and infrared (IR) range — about 99 per cent of the Sun’s energy falls within these wavelengths.

The text explains that these three regions of the spectrum shape the Earth’s climate and support life.

The text explains that gamma rays and X-rays are mostly filtered by the Earth’s upper atmosphere, while microwaves and radio waves carry very little energy to significantly warm the Earth.

The text explains that short wavelength UV radiation is mostly absorbed by the ozone layer in the upper atmosphere, protecting life and contributing to some atmospheric heating.

The text explains that visible light from the Sun reaches the Earth’s surface and provides energy for photosynthesis, which is the primary source of food for most organisms.

The text explains that infrared radiation warms the Earth’s surface, which then re-radiates this heat back into the atmosphere.

The text explains that a portion of this outgoing heat is trapped by the greenhouse gases, such as carbon dioxide (CO2), methane (CH4) and water vapour, keeping the Earth warm enough to support life.

The text explains that the UV rays lie in the wavelength range of 100 nm to 400 nm [1 nanometre (nm) = 10–9 m] and have a much higher energy than the visible light.

The text explains that ready to Go Beyond The amount of the Sun’s radiation that reaches the Earth’s surface is called insolation.

The text explains that it is responsible for warming the Earth’s surface and its atmosphere.

The text explains that the average amount of solar energy received per unit time per unit area that is perpendicular to the Sun’s rays at the top of the Earth’s atmosphere is called the solar constant.

The text explains that it represents the Sun’s energy available on the Earth before any absorption, scattering or reflection occurs in the atmosphere.

The text explains that 13.2: Electromagnetic spectrum UV IR Radio waves Microwaves X-rays Gamma rays higher frequency or shorter wavelength lower frequency or longer wavelength Visible

The text explains that 255 Earth as a System: Energy, Matter, and L ife The solar constant is important because it helps scientists understand the Earth’s energy balance, climate and weather patterns.

The text explains that however, some of this energy is absorbed and scattered by gases, clouds and dust particles in the atmosphere before reaching the surface of the Earth.

The text explains that (i) tends to give 1 electron (ii) Cation (A+) (iii) tends to take 2 electrons (iv) Anion (B2–) (v) Ionic (vi) A2B 3.

The text explains that (i) CaBr2 (ii) Al2(CO3)3 (iii) K2SO4 (iv) NH4Cl 7.

The text explains that (i) Mg3N2 (ii) Li3N (iii) Na2S (iv) Al2O3 10.

The text explains that nO 3 – SO 4 2− PO 4 3− NH 4 + NH 4 NO 3 (NH 4 ) 2 SO 4 (NH 4 ) 3 PO 4 Li+ LiNO 3 Li2 SO 4 Li3 PO 4 Al 3+ Al(NO 3 ) 3 Al 2 (SO 4 ) 3 AlPO 4 Cu2+ Cu(NO 3 ) 2 CuSO4 Cu3 (PO 4 ) 2 12.

The text explains that (i) Z = 11; A = 23 (ii) Cation (iii) Electronic configuration 2, 8 (iv) Sodium cation (Na+) 13.

The text explains that electrons 13, Neutrons 14; Electrons 36, Neutrons 45; Electrons 78, Neutrons 121 Chapter 10 Pause and Ponder 3.

The text explains that a - Green curve; B - Red curve; C - Blue curve 9.

The text explains that correct sequence is (iii), (i), (ii), (iv) 3.

The text explains that tomato—self pollination; wheat—self pollination; papaya—cross pollination Chapter 12 Revise, Reflect, Refine 1.

The text explains that (i) Q (ii) P, characterised by no true nucleus (iii) both can be classified based on level of organisation, the organism Q is multicellular and organism R is unicellular (v) There is no place for acellular entities Chapter 13 Revise, Reflect, Refine 1.

Source: NCERT Class 9 Science, Chapter 13: Why Do We Fall Ill
